logistics.explanation: Multi-day format spans cities like Mangshi, Tengchong, Fengqing, Pu’er, and Xishuangbanna with included bus transfers between stages. Organizers handle accommodations and meals for up to 2000 riders across two weekends in November. International participants benefit from English support but face visa logistics to remote Southwest China.

length.explanation: Single-day approximation of 87 miles reflects segments of the 650+ km multi-stage total across five stages. Riders tackle looping routes daily in cities like Dehong-Mangshi to Xishuangbanna-Jinghong. This distance challenges amateurs over dramatic terrain without ultra-endurance extremes.

technicality.explanation: Paved roads through tea plantations and ethnic villages demand standard road handling on descents after climbs. No gravel or singletrack; focus is on positioning in packs of 1600+ riders during flats like the 40 km Mangshi stage. Descents from ascents require confident cornering amid mountain views.

elevation.explanation: 7500 feet gain mirrors intense climbing across Yunnan’s mountains, akin to the event’s total 12,300 m over stages. Riders face steady ascents up to 20% grades before looping descents in areas like Tengchong and Pu’er. This tests endurance on multi-day efforts through highland passes.

climate.explanation: November brings mild Southwest China weather ideal for long rides, avoiding summer heat and monsoon rains. Stages through tea plantations enjoy clear skies and uplifting mountain air near Myanmar and Thailand borders. Occasional weather tweaks routes, as seen in past rainy stages.

altitude.explanation: Yunnan’s high plateaus around Kunming and stage cities like Lincang introduce moderate altitude effects for sea-level riders. Climbs add to breathing challenges amid ethnic minority villages. Acclimatization over multi-days helps manage the 1000-2000m elevations.

adventure.explanation: Multi-stage journey from Mangshi near Myanmar to Xishuangbanna near Thailand immerses riders in tea plantations and ethnic cultures. Closed roads enable safe exploration of five prefectures with cultural stops. Dramatic scenery and village descents elevate it beyond standard sportives.

prestige.explanation: China’s largest amateur multi-day event since 2014 draws 2000 riders but remains regional in global context. Rapid growth to 2100 registrants in 45 minutes shows domestic popularity. Offers gold/silver/bronze certificates for stage completions.

race_quality.explanation: Well-organized with closed roads, team competitions, and Duo TT prologue in Mangshi for 1000 riders. Nordic Ways manages buses, meals, and GC across five stages reliably. Sells out fast, ensuring smooth execution for 1600+ starters.

experience.explanation: Exceptional venues like Dianchi Lake in Kunming and Lijiang routes blend cycling with culinary and cultural immersion. Cheering crowds and pack racing amid pure scenery create infectious atmosphere. Multi-day format mimics pro tours for hobbyists.

community.explanation: Brings together domestic and international cyclists in massive 2100-rider fields across China. Teams from Rafa Shanghai foster camaraderie during stages in Baoshan and Lincang. Shared challenges in ethnic villages build strong bonds.

field_depth.explanation: Around 2000 participants including max 1000 for full long-distance GC, with strong amateur and young talent fields. Slots fill in 45 minutes, mixing pros and hobbyists in 1600-rider starts. Domestic depth dominates regional scene.

value.explanation: Includes buses, meals, and multi-stage access for entry fee, delivering high adventure in scenic Yunnan. Covers five cities’ highlights like Fengqing tea areas at accessible cost for 650+ km. Exceptional for cultural-cycling combo.

expenses.explanation: Travel to remote Mangshi or Jinghong adds flight and visa costs from major hubs. On-site logistics covered, but multi-day stay inflates total for internationals. Domestic riders get strong value from included transfers.

cultural_impact.explanation: Pioneered non-pro multi-stage racing in China since 2014, boosting Southwest cycling. Showcases ethnic minorities and tea plantations in Dehong to Xishuangbanna. Holds regional significance as largest amateur event.

course_description.summary: Granfondo Yunnan features paved roads through Southwest China’s dramatic mountains, tea plantations, and ethnic minority villages. The approximate 87-mile single-day profile captures a stage’s essence from the 650+ km multi-stage event. Riders experience flat city starts, steep climbs, and thrilling descents in iconic areas like Mangshi and Tengchong.

key_segments.name: Mangshi Flat Sprint
key_segments.description: Fast 40 km city roads near Myanmar border kick off with pack pacing at 40 km/h averages. Ideal for positioning among 1600 riders before climbs begin.

key_segments.name: Tengchong Mountain Climb
key_segments.description: Steep ascents through highland passes up to 20% gradients amid tea plantations and villages. Followed by looping descents testing bike handling.

key_segments.name: Pu’er Descent Finale
key_segments.description: Rolling finish toward Xishuangbanna with cultural village views and recovery flats. Key for GC time after cumulative elevation.

course_description.elevation_profile: Profile features repeated punchy climbs and descents totaling 7500 feet, mimicking stage totals toward 12,300 m event-wide. Early flats yield to mid-stage mountains around 20% pitches, then looping drops. Constant up-down suits strong climbers in Yunnan highlands.
course_description.aid_stations: Aid stations at stage midpoints and ends provide hydration, local snacks, and meals; full catering included for multi-day recovery.

travel.accommodation: Organizers provide hotels in stage cities like Mangshi and Pu’er; camping unavailable due to multi-day format.
travel.bike_transport: Rentals available via partners like Serk Cycling; shipping to Kunming easy, with event transport between stages.

race_day.categories: long distance GC, shorter options, male/female/mixed Duo TT, age groups, teams
race_day.awards: GC podiums, certificates by time, team rankings

training_recommendations.minimum_fitness: Build base for 87 miles with 5000 ft gain; complete century rides and hill repeats weekly for 2 months prior.
training_recommendations.ideal_preparation: Simulate multi-day with back-to-back 70-90 mile rides including 6000+ ft elevation over weekends. Focus on 4-5 hour endurance at 200-250W normalized power. Include descent practice and pack riding drills for Yunnan stages.
training_recommendations.key_workouts: 3x20 min at 4w/kg on 10-20% grades; 5-hour flat tempo at 40 km/h; 2-day tourer with 8000 ft gain total.

history.notable_facts: Inaugural five-stage event across Kunming, Yuxi, Chuxiong revolutionized Chinese amateur cycling. Expanded to new cities like Mangshi and Ruili in 2018. Grew to 2100 riders by 2025, selling out in 45 minutes despite COVID single-stage in 2020.

final_verdict.one_liner: China’s premier amateur stage race blends epic Yunnan climbs with cultural immersion.
final_verdict.who_should_ride: Strong amateurs seeking multi-day road challenges amid tea hills and villages. Ideal for those craving Asian adventure with 2000-rider energy.
final_verdict.who_should_skip: Novices daunted by 7500 ft days or remote travel logistics. Skip if altitude or visa hassles deter.
final_verdict.insider_tip: Start mid-pack in Mangshi stage to draft through flats and save legs for Tengchong climbs.

suffering_zones.label: Tea Plantation Punches
suffering_zones.description: Endless 15-20% switchbacks through Pu’er and Fengqing plantations sap legs after flats. Heat and views tempt easing off, but GC demands push.

suffering_zones.label: Highland Descent Gauntlet
suffering_zones.description: Technical drops from Tengchong passes past ethnic villages test brakes and nerves amid 1600-rider traffic. Cumulative fatigue peaks here.

suffering_zones.label: Multi-Day Accumulation
suffering_zones.description: Day 3-5 in Lincang-Xishuangbanna compounds 12,000m total gain, hitting recovery walls despite aid.
